en.extract:
The Asian barred owlet (Glaucidium cuculoides), also known as the cuckoo owlet, is a species of pygmy owl native to the forests and shrublands of mainland Southeast Asia to the foothills of the Himalayas of northern Pakistan. It is a smaller owl, measuring 22–25 cm (8.7–9.8 in), making it one of the larger pygmy owls. They are primarily insectivorous but will eat lizards, small rodents and birds as well.


Description
The Asian barred owlet is a small owl that sits at 22–25 cm (8.7–9.8 in). The males weigh 150–176 g (0.331–0.388 lb) and the females up to 240 g (0.53 lb). Like most owls in the true owl family, the females are usually larger than the males. They are dark brown or olive-brown with a white throat patch and are densely barred. Their breast and belly is whitish in colour with dark brown bars. Its tail and wings are dark brown with whitish bars. Unlike other pygmy owl species, they do not have false eyes on the back of their head. The juveniles have a streaked breast, not barred, and their head is a more ruddy brown colour. They are similar in appearance to the Javan owlet and collared owlet. The Asian barred owlet is mainly diurnal and will perch on bare branches to hunt freely throughout the day. Being more active during the day, they can often be mobbed by smaller birds and will sit still during the mobbing. Similar to other owlets, they have an undulating flight pattern, a series of rapid flaps then a pause with closed wings.


Taxonomy
The Asian barred owl (Glaucidium cuculoides) is part of the Strigidae family, also known as the true owls. Within the family, there are 28 genera with 194 species. The genus Glaucidium includes the pygmy owls and is closely related to the genus Athene. The genus Glaucidium includes 26 species, such as the Asian barred owlet. The species was first described in 1831 and named Noctua cuculoides before being reclassified to the genus Glaucidium. On occasion, the name Taenioglaux cuculoides has been used synonymously to Glaucidium cuculoides when referring to the Asian barred owlet. There are currently eight recognized subspecies of the Asian barred owlet:

G. c. cuculoides (Vigors, 1830) – west, central Himalayas
G. c. austerum Ripley, 1948 – northeast India, Bhutan and northwest Myanmar
G. c. rufescens Baker, ECS, 1926 – northeast India, Bangladesh, north Myanmar and south Yunnan (south China)
G. c. whiteleyi (Blyth, 1867) – south China to northeast Vietnam
G. c. persimile Hartert, EJO, 1910 – Hainan (off south China)
G. c. delacouri Ripley, 1948 – northeast Laos and northwest, central Vietnam
G. c. deignani Ripley, 1948 – southeast Thailand, south Vietnam and Cambodia
G. c. bruegeli (Parrot, 1908) – southeast Myanmar and Thailand (except southeast)


Habitat and distribution

Asian barred owlets are commonly found in woodland habitats such as forests of pine and oak, subtropical and tropical evergreen jungles at lower elevations. They can also be found in foothills to submontane regions 2,100 m (6,900 ft) in elevation. The highest recorded sighting was at 2,700 m (8,900 ft) in the Himalayas of north Pakistan. They also sometimes live around developed areas in gardens and parks. Asian barred owlets have also displayed a preference for secondary forests over primary forests in parts of their range.
Asian barred owlets are widespread in the mainland of southeast Asia. They can be found in southeast China, Bhutan, Myanmar, Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, Nepal, Bangladesh, northeast India and the foothills of the Himalayas into Pakistan.


Behaviour


Vocalizations
The male song is a trill [1] lasting anywhere from 5–20 seconds, progressively becoming louder and harsher notes before stopping abruptly, transcribed as "kwuhk kwuhk-ke-kwuhk kwuhk-kekekwurre kwurre kwurre-kwurrekwurrekwurre kwurrekwurr". They also have a common call which is a smooth "hoot". Asian barred owlets are noisiest at dawn and a couple of hours after sunrise but will vocalize at any time of day.


Diet
Their diet mainly consists of insects, such as beetles, grasshoppers and cicadas. They will also eat lizards, small rodents, such as mice, and small birds. They hunt from a perch and will catch birds in the air. They have been observed catching common quail (Coturnix coturnix) in flight, snatching them from the air if they fly past.


Reproduction
The breeding season of Asian barred owlets is from January to February in Thailand and March to June in other parts of their range. They nest in unlined tree cavities and old nests of woodpeckers and barbets. They can also use the old nest cavity of collared scops owls (Otus lettia) where they share a habitat. They have even been known to kill woodpeckers or barbets for a nest hole. There have been a few ground nests observed in Thailand back in 2017, which were possibly a result of no suitable nest sites in the area. A breeding pair will lay a single clutch of 3 to 5 white and rounded eggs in a year. Once hatched, both parents take care of the young and bring food to the nest. The chicks will fledge as early as April in Thailand and early July for the majority of their range.


Movement
Asian barred owlets are resident birds and do not migrate long distances. They will move locally and tend to make local elevational movements by moving to lower elevations in the non-breeding season.


Relationship with people
In parts of the Asian barred owlet's range, owls are seen as an evil bird and a sign of bad news to see or hear. This belief leads people to attempt to eradicate owls by shooting them, stealing eggs and using pesticides to kill them. These birds are already under threat from habitat destruction due to urbanization and deforestation so the consequences of this prejudice just increases their risk and discourages local conservation efforts.


References


External links
 Media related to Glaucidium cuculoides at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Glaucidium cuculoides at Wikispecies
BirdLife species factsheet for Glaucidium cuculoides
"Glaucidium cuculoides". Avibase. 
"Asian barred owlet media". Internet Bird Collection.
Asian barred owlet photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Interactive range map of Glaucidium cuculoides at IUCN Red List
Audio recordings of Asian barred owlet on Xeno-canto.

The '''Asian barred owlet''' ('''''Glaucidium cuculoides'''''), also known as the '''cuckoo owlet''', is a species of [[pygmy owl]] native to the forests and shrublands of mainland [[Southeast Asia]] to the foothills of the [[Himalayas]] of [[northern Pakistan]]. It is a smaller owl, measuring {{Convert|22-25|cm|in|abbr=on}}, making it one of the larger pygmy owls. They are primarily [[insectivorous]] but will eat lizards, small rodents and birds as well.

== Description ==
The Asian barred owlet is a small owl that sits at {{Convert|22-25|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Sarkar |first1=Aritri |last2=Sailas |first2=S. Sangeeth |last3=Babu |first3=Santhanakrishnan |last4=Karunakaran |first4=P. V. |last5=Kumara |first5=Honnavalli N. |last6=Shanmugavel |first6=Sureshmarimuthu |last7=Pramod |first7=Padmanaban |date=2022-12-19 |title=Community Reserves Serve as Refugia Sites for Cavity-Nesting Owls in Northeast India |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=57 |issue=1 |pages=22–29 |doi=10.3356/JRR-21-48 |issn=0892-1016}}</ref> The males weigh {{Convert|150-176|g|lb|abbr=on}} and the females up to {{Convert|240|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Sankamethawee |first1=Wangworn |last2=Pierce |first2=Andrew J |date=2019 |title=Ground-nesting attempt by asian barred owlet glaucidium cuculoides |journal=Natural History Bulletin of the Siam Society |volume=63 |issue=2 |pages=157–160}}</ref> Like most owls in the [[Strigidae|true owl]] family, the females are usually larger than the males. They are dark brown or olive-brown with a white throat patch and are densely barred. Their breast and belly is whitish in colour with dark brown bars. Its tail and wings are dark brown with whitish bars. Unlike other [[pygmy owl]] species, they do not have false eyes on the back of their head. The juveniles have a streaked breast, not barred, and their head is a more ruddy brown colour. They are similar in appearance to the [[Javan owlet]] and [[collared owlet]]. The Asian barred owlet is mainly diurnal and will perch on bare branches to hunt freely throughout the day. Being more active during the day, they can often be mobbed by smaller birds and will sit still during the mobbing. Similar to other owlets, they have an undulating flight pattern, a series of rapid flaps then a pause with closed wings.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last1=König |first1=Claus |title=Owls of the world |last2=Weick |first2=Friedhelm |last3=Becking |first3=Jan-Hendrick |publisher=Bloomsbury |year=2008 |isbn=978-0-7136-6548-2 |edition=2nd |series=Helm identification guides |location=London |pages=699–702}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The Asian barred owl (''Glaucidium cuculoides)'' is part of the [[Strigidae]] family, also known as the true owls.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Cheng-He |last2=Liu |first2=Hong-Yi |last3=Min |first3=Xiao |last4=Lu |first4=Chang-Hu |date=2020-02-22 |title=Mitogenome of the little owl Athene noctua and phylogenetic analysis of Strigidae |journal=International Journal of Biological Macromolecules |volume=151 |issue=1 |pages=924–931 |doi=10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.02.238 |pmid=32097733 |issn=0141-8130}}</ref> Within the family, there are 28 genera with 194 species.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Salter |first1=Jessie F |last2=Oliveros |first2=Carl H |last3=Hosner |first3=Peter A |last4=Manthey |first4=Joseph D |last5=Robbins |first5=Mark B |last6=Moyle |first6=Robert G |last7=Brumfield |first7=Robb T |last8=Faircloth |first8=Brant C |date=2019-12-12 |title=Extensive paraphyly in the typical owl family (Strigidae) |journal=The Auk |volume=137 |issue=1 |pages=1–15 |doi=10.1093/auk/ukz070 |issn=0004-8038|doi-access=free |hdl=2346/93048 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> The [[Glaucidium (bird)|genus ''Glaucidium'']] includes the pygmy owls and is closely related to the [[Athene (bird)|genus ''Athene'']].<ref name=":1" /> The genus ''Glaucidium'' includes 26 species, such as the Asian barred owlet. The species was first described in 1831 and named ''Noctua cuculoides'' before being reclassified to the genus ''Glaucidium''.<ref name=":0" /> On occasion, the name ''Taenioglaux cuculoides'' has been used synonymously to ''Glaucidium cuculoides'' when referring to the Asian barred owlet.<ref name=":0" /> There are currently eight recognized [[subspecies]] of the Asian barred owlet:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=Owls | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/owls/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=25 October 2023 }}</ref>

* ''G. c. cuculoides'' ([[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Vigors]], 1830) – west, central Himalayas
* ''G. c. austerum'' [[Sidney Dillon Ripley|Ripley]], 1948 – northeast India, Bhutan and northwest Myanmar
* ''G. c. rufescens'' [[E. C. Stuart Baker|Baker, ECS]], 1926 – northeast India, Bangladesh, north Myanmar and south [[Yunnan]] (south China)
* ''G. c. whiteleyi'' ([[Edward Blyth|Blyth]], 1867) – south China to northeast Vietnam
* ''G. c. persimile'' [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1910 – [[Hainan]] (off south China)
* ''G. c. delacouri'' Ripley, 1948 – northeast Laos and northwest, central Vietnam
* ''G. c. deignani'' Ripley, 1948 – southeast Thailand, south Vietnam and Cambodia
* ''G. c. bruegeli'' ([[Carl Parrot|Parrot]], 1908) – southeast Myanmar and Thailand (except southeast)

== Habitat and distribution ==
[[File:Asian Barred Owlet in Kaziranga National Park March 2025 by Tisha Mukherjee 01.jpg|thumb|In [[Kaziranga National Park]], Assam, India.]]
Asian barred owlets are commonly found in [[woodland]] habitats such as forests of pine and oak, subtropical and tropical evergreen jungles at lower elevations. They can also be found in foothills to submontane regions {{Convert|2100|m|ft|abbr=on}} in elevation.<ref name=":0" /> The highest recorded sighting was at {{Convert|2700|m|ft|abbr=on}} in the [[Himalayas]] of north [[Pakistan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Rasmussen |first1=Pamela C. |title=Birds of South Asia: the Ripley Guide |last2=Anderton |first2=John C. |last3=Alderfer |first3=Jonathan |last4=Burn |first4=Hilary |last5=Gilbert |first5=Albert E. |date=2005 |publisher=Lynx Ed |others=Smithsonian Institution |isbn=978-84-87334-67-2 |edition=1. Aufl. |location=Barcelona}}</ref> They also sometimes live around developed areas in gardens and parks.<ref name=":0" /> Asian barred owlets have also displayed a preference for [[secondary forest]]s over [[primary forest]]s in parts of their range.<ref name=":2" />

Asian barred owlets are widespread in the mainland of [[southeast Asia]]. They can be found in southeast [[China]], [[Bhutan]], [[Myanmar]], [[Laos]], [[Vietnam]], [[Thailand]], [[Nepal]], [[Bangladesh]], northeast [[India]] and the foothills of the [[Himalayas]] into [[Pakistan]].<ref name=":3" />

== Behaviour ==

=== Vocalizations ===
The male song is a trill [https://xeno-canto.org/328317] lasting anywhere from 5–20 seconds, progressively becoming louder and harsher notes before stopping abruptly, transcribed as "{{Not a typo|kwuhk kwuhk-ke-kwuhk kwuhk-kekekwurre kwurre kwurre-kwurrekwurrekwurre kwurrekwurr}}". They also have a common call which is a smooth "hoot". Asian barred owlets are noisiest at dawn and a couple of hours after sunrise but will vocalize at any time of day.<ref name=":0" />

=== Diet ===
Their diet mainly consists of insects, such as beetles, grasshoppers and cicadas. They will also eat lizards, small rodents, such as mice, and small birds. They hunt from a perch and will catch birds in the air. They have been observed catching [[common quail]] (''Coturnix coturnix'') in flight, snatching them from the air if they fly past.<ref name=":0" />

=== Reproduction ===
The breeding season of Asian barred owlets is from January to February in Thailand and March to June in other parts of their range.<ref name=":3" /> They nest in unlined tree cavities and old nests of [[woodpecker]]s and [[Asian barbet|barbets]].<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":0" /> They can also use the old nest cavity of [[collared scops owl]]s (''Otus lettia'') where they share a habitat.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Leadprathom |first1=Kumron |last2=Chimchome |first2=Vijak |last3=Bumrungsri |first3=Sara |date=2009-12-01 |title=Nesting Ecology of the Collared Scops Owl ''Otus lettia''in Thailand |journal=Ardea |volume=97 |issue=4 |pages=457–461 |doi=10.5253/078.097.0409 |issn=0373-2266|doi-access=free }}</ref> They have even been known to kill woodpeckers or barbets for a nest hole.<ref name=":0" /> There have been a few ground nests observed in Thailand back in 2017, which were possibly a result of no suitable nest sites in the area.<ref name=":3" /> A breeding pair will lay a single clutch of 3 to 5 white and rounded eggs in a year.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":0" /> Once hatched, both parents take care of the young and bring food to the nest.<ref name=":0" /> The chicks will fledge as early as April in Thailand and early July for the majority of their range.<ref name=":3" />

=== Movement ===
Asian barred owlets are resident birds and do not migrate long distances. They will move locally and tend to make local elevational movements by moving to lower elevations in the non-breeding season.<ref name=":0" />

== Relationship with people ==
In parts of the Asian barred owlet's range, owls are seen as an evil bird and a sign of bad news to see or hear. This belief leads people to attempt to eradicate owls by shooting them, stealing eggs and using pesticides to kill them. These birds are already under threat from habitat destruction due to urbanization and deforestation so the consequences of this prejudice just increases their risk and discourages local conservation efforts.<ref name=":4" />

zh.extract:
斑頭鵂鶹（學名：Glaucidium cuculoides）是一種無角羽的貓頭鷹。頭部圓形，站姿比其他小型貓頭鷹更接近水平方向。棲息於森林裏面，會於樹洞築巢。常於早晨時段發出似裝滿水的瓶子倒轉時的急促咯咯叫聲，主要捕捉昆蟲、老鼠，以及其他較小隻的貓頭鷹來吃。
斑頭鵂鶹是香港的常見留鳥，受動植物（瀕危物種保護）條例（第586章）保護。


亞種及其分布
斑頭鵂鶹主要分布在孟加拉、印度、中國（包括香港、海南島）、泰國等亞洲地區。過去可以分5個亞種：尼泊爾亞種cuculoides腹部的白色羽毛範圍不大，前胸同腹部有深色的橫斑；persimile是海南島亞種，全身體羽較偏紅棕色。香港品種是whiteleyi亞種。現時本物種包括下列八個亞種：

Glaucidium cuculoides austerum Ripley, 1948
Glaucidium cuculoides bruegeli (Parrot, 1908)
Glaucidium cuculoides cuculoides (Vigors, 1831)
Glaucidium cuculoides deignani Ripley, 1948
Glaucidium cuculoides delacouri Ripley, 1948
Glaucidium cuculoides persimile Hartert, 1910
Glaucidium cuculoides rufescens E. C. S. Baker, 1926
Glaucidium cuculoides whitelyi (Blyth, 1867)
